ocr.text: The table titled "HEV Infections in Developing Countries and Tropical Countries" presents a comprehensive overview of the
distribution of Hepatitis E virus genotypes (GT) across various regions in 2016, highlighting significant disparities in infection
rates. Specifically, the data outlines the prevalence of GT 1, predominantly found in Asia and parts of Africa, and GT 2, which
is primarily observed in Mexico and certain regions of Africa. Conversely, GT 3, which is more widespread across the USA,
South America, and Europe, and occasionally detected in Asia, exhibits a different epidemiological pattern.
HEV Infections in Developing Countries  HEV Infections in Tropical Countries
Genotypes (GT) (distribution)  GT 1(Asia, parts of Africa) and GT 2 (Mexico, parts of Africa)  GT 3 (USA, South America, Europe, sometimes in Asia) and 4 (Asia, rarely in Europe)

Severity  Imported infections seem to be more severe than autochthonous ones in Europe [11]. More than 16% of HEV GT 1 or 2 infections result in clinical apparent hepatitis E [12,13].  Less severe than imported infections [11]. GT 4 infections eventually less severe than GT 3 infections [14,15]. Less than 2% of HEV infections in industrialized nations lead to clinical apparent hepatitis E [16,17]
Risk groups for acute or acute-on-chronic liver failure  Pregnant women are at risk for development of acute liver failure [12]  Elderly men, patients with underlying liver diseases [18,19]

Risk groups for chronic HEV infections  No chronic hepatitis E caused by GT 1 or 2  GT 3 and 4 can cause chronic hepatitis E in immunosuppressed individuals (transplant recipients, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-infected patients, patients with hereditary or acquired immunological disturbances, haematological patients)
Endemicity  Endemic: Outbreaks during conditions of decreased hygienic conditions [3,20]  Sporadic: rare symptomatic cases, but diagnosed with emerging frequency within the last years
Year  Gasoline(Mt)  Diesel Oil(Mt)  Natural Gas(billion m3)  Kerosene, Crude Oil, Fuel Oil (Mt)
Reaction time
Amount of precursor  10 min  20 min
The metrics provided in the table, such as "Gasoline (Mt)" and "Diesel Oil (Mt)", not only reflect the energy consumption
landscape of these regions but may also indirectly correlate with HEV transmission dynamics. The year 2016 serves as a pivotal
reference point, allowing for a comparative analysis of HEV infection patterns in relation to environmental and socio-economic
factors, including fuel consumption and the availability of healthcare resources. This context underscores the multifaceted
nature of HEV epidemiology and its association with regional developmental factors.
